quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: Develops and refines food formulations through product trials, sensory evaluation, testing, packaging review and market-readiness work, while considering hygiene, labelling and food-safety requirements.

quick_facts.label: Regulatory context in India
quick_facts.value: Food-product development must be aligned with the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 and the applicable FSSAI product-standard, additive, contaminant, packaging and labelling/display regulations.

quick_facts.label: Relevant parent qualification
quick_facts.value: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is a four-year professional degree that includes Foods and Nutrition among its core areas; ICAR minimum requirements identify Food Science and Nutrition as a department within Community Science.

quick_facts.label: Further-learning option
quick_facts.value: At least one agricultural university lists Community Science graduates as eligible for its postgraduate diploma in Post-Harvest Technology, Food Safety and Quality, subject to that university's admission rules.

quick_facts.label: Skills ecosystem
quick_facts.value: FICSI, the food-processing sector skill council, publishes National Occupational Standards and Qualification Packs; its active list was stated as last updated on 17 March 2026.

sections.heading: What a Food Product Development Executive does
paragraphs: This role supports the route from a product idea to a reproducible food product. Typical work includes preparing and adjusting formulations; running small-scale trials; evaluating taste, texture, appearance and other sensory attributes; documenting test results; reviewing packaging; and improving prototypes before market launch. Food-development decisions also need to account for product stability, quality and shelf life.

In India, the work is compliance-sensitive: the FSSAI framework covers food-product standards and additives, contaminants, packaging and labelling/display requirements. The applicable requirements depend on the product category and claims, so the executive normally works with quality, regulatory and production colleagues rather than treating product development as a purely culinary task.
bullets: Assist with formulation, prototype preparation and repeat trials.
Use sensory evaluation and product testing to compare variants and support standardisation.
Maintain clear records of ingredients, process changes, test observations and product specifications.
Coordinate with quality, packaging, production and regulatory functions on safety, specifications and launch readiness.

sections.heading: Entry route from B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science
paragraphs: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is a relevant foundation because Food and Nutrition is a core area of the degree, and ICAR's minimum requirements for the discipline include a Department of Food Science and Nutrition. A student aiming at product-development work should deliberately build practical evidence alongside the degree: food-processing or value-addition projects, formulation trials, sensory evaluation, basic food analysis, shelf-life work and documentation.

Short courses or structured skilling can supplement a degree, but candidates should verify that a course and its assessment are current. FICSI describes its standards as National Occupational Standards and Qualification Packs, while the Ministry of Food Processing Industries identifies FICSI as the sector skill council collaborating on food-processing curricula. For further specialisation, university-specific postgraduate routes can include food safety, quality and post-harvest technology; eligibility and admission rules vary by institution and year.
bullets: Use Foods and Nutrition coursework to build grounding in ingredients, nutrition and food safety.
Seek internships or project work in university food-processing, incubation, bakery, quality-analysis or value-addition facilities.
Build a portfolio containing trial sheets, sensory-score summaries, standard recipes/formulations and concise product-specification documents.
Consider institution-specific postgraduate or skill credentials only after checking the current eligibility, curriculum and validity details.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The practical skill set combines food science with disciplined testing and communication. FICSI's new-food-product-development material highlights the effect of ingredients on taste, nutrition, texture, shelf life and stability, as well as prototype preparation, sensory trials, internal testing, packaging review, labelling basics and hygiene. University food-analysis and consultancy facilities similarly identify value addition, new product development, quality analysis and shelf-life testing as connected capabilities.

The role also requires regulatory literacy. Product teams need to identify which FSSAI standards apply to their food category and coordinate on compliant ingredients, additives, contaminants, packaging and labels. This is best understood as working knowledge for development decisions, not a substitute for formal regulatory review where required.
bullets: Formulation and ingredient-function knowledge.
Sensory evaluation, sample preparation and trial design.
Food-quality, stability and shelf-life testing awareness.
Accurate technical documentation and standardisation discipline.
Packaging and label-review awareness.
Working knowledge of applicable FSSAI product and safety requirements.

sections.heading: Where the work is carried out
paragraphs: Food product development is commonly conducted across product-development kitchens or pilot areas, food-analysis laboratories, packaging and quality functions, and production-facing teams. A university Community Science food-and-nutrition unit illustrates these connected settings by providing bakery equipment, food analysis, and consultancy in value addition, new food product development, quality analysis and shelf-life testing for entrepreneurs and food-processing industrialists.

The employer context can range from established food manufacturers to nutrition-oriented enterprises and early-stage food businesses. In each setting, the executive's work is usually collaborative: development outcomes must be translated into repeatable specifications and production-compatible processes while meeting food-safety requirements.
bullets: R&D or product-development kitchens and pilot-scale trial areas.
Food-quality and food-analysis laboratories.
Food-manufacturing plants, packaging functions and quality teams.
Food-business incubators, technical-consultancy units and entrepreneur-led processing ventures.

sections.heading: How the role can develop
paragraphs: Career development can move toward greater ownership of formulations, standardisation, quality and food-safety coordination, or toward specialised food-and-nutrition work. Relevant postgraduate study and research areas listed by an agricultural university include food safety and quality assurance, sustainable food systems, food fortification, functional foods and nutraceuticals. The exact title and seniority path depend on the employer's product category, scale and organisation structure.

A portfolio of documented development work is especially useful for progression: evidence can include prototype iterations, sensory results, quality or shelf-life observations, standard operating notes and successful hand-off of a product specification to production or business teams.
bullets: Develop deeper responsibility for product trials and standardisation.
Specialise in quality assurance, food safety, fortification, functional foods or nutraceuticals.
Pursue relevant postgraduate study where eligibility requirements are met.
Use documented project outcomes to demonstrate readiness for broader product or technical responsibility.
